title: Swietenia mahagoni
taxonomy: Swietenia mahagoni is classified as a species of tree within the family Meliaceae. It belongs to the genus Swietenia, which is native to the Americas. The species is also known as West Indian mahogany or Cuban mahogany. Swietenia mahagoni is a deciduous tree that can grow up to 60 meters tall. The tree is valued for its timber, which is durable and resistant to decay.
uses: Swietenia mahagoni, commonly known as the West Indian mahogany, has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits. The plant's bark, leaves, and seeds contain compounds with anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties. In some cultures, it is used to treat fever, skin conditions, and respiratory issues, as well as to alleviate symptoms of gastrointestinal problems. Additionally, the plant's extracts have been studied for their potential to protect against certain cancers and to boost the immune system. Further research is needed to fully understand the medicinal properties and applications of Swietenia mahagoni.

side_effects: Swietenia mahagoni, also known as West Indian mahogany, contains sapogenins and saponins, which can cause gastrointestinal issues, such as nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea, when ingested in large quantities. The sap of the tree can also cause skin irritation and allergic reactions in some individuals. intro: Swietenia mahagoni, also known as the West Indian mahogany, is a large, evergreen tree native to the Caribbean and Central America. The plant is known for its numerous health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which have been traditionally used to treat various ailments such as fever, rheumatism, and respiratory infections. The bioactive constituents of Swietenia mahagoni include flavonoids, terpenoids, and alkaloids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties.
